questions.q: By Fermat–Euler, $7^{40} \bmod 100$ equals which of the following?
Which integer is a primitive root modulo $7$?
questions.explain: Powers of $3$ mod $7$ cycle through $3,2,6,4,5,1$ — all six nonzero residues. The order of $3$ is $\phi(7)=6$, so $3$ is primitive. ($2$ has order $3$; $6\equiv -1$ has order $2$.)

questions.q: RSA toy: $p=11,q=13$, so $n=143$ and $\phi(n)=120$. Take $e=7$. Compute the private exponent $d=e^{-1} \bmod 120$.
Why is RSA decryption $c^d\equiv m \pmod n$ correct?
choices: Because $ed\equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so $m^{ed}=m^{1+k\phi(n)}\equiv m \pmod n$ by Fermat–Euler
Because $d$ is the multiplicative inverse of $c$ mod $n$
Because every $m$ is a primitive root mod $n$
questions.explain: The key identity is $ed\equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ (not mod $n$); Fermat–Euler then collapses $m^{k\phi(n)}$ to $1$ when $\gcd(m,n)=1$, and a CRT argument handles the remaining cases.

questions.q: Knowing $\phi(n)$ for an RSA modulus $n=pq$ is equivalent to knowing what?
questions.explain: From $n=pq$ and $\phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)=n-(p+q)+1$ one recovers $p+q$, and combined with $pq=n$ this gives a quadratic whose roots are $p$ and $q$. So $\phi(n)$ and the factorization are polynomial-time equivalent.

questions.q: In $(\mathbb{Z}/23)^\times$ with primitive root $g=5$, find $\log_5 10$ (i.e. the smallest $x\ge 0$ with $5^x \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$).
In Diffie–Hellman, Alice sends $A=g^a$, Bob sends $B=g^b$. The shared secret is:
choices: $g^{ab}$, computed by Alice as $B^a$ and by Bob as $A^b$
questions.explain: Both parties compute $g^{ab}$: Alice raises Bob's $B=g^b$ to her secret $a$, Bob raises $A=g^a$ to his secret $b$. The eavesdropper sees $g^a, g^b$ and faces the computational Diffie–Hellman problem to recover $g^{ab}$.

questions.q: What is the asymptotic complexity of the index calculus for the discrete log in $\mathbb{F}_p^\times$?
questions.explain: Index calculus runs in sub-exponential time $L_p[1/3,c]=\exp((c+o(1))(\log p)^{1/3}(\log\log p)^{2/3})$. This is faster than Pollard's $\rho$ ($O(\sqrt p)$) on $\mathbb{F}_p^\times$ but exploits the smooth-factorization structure unique to the multiplicative group of a field.

questions.q: Why does ECC achieve smaller key sizes than finite-field DH for the same security level?
choices: Elliptic-curve points are encoded with fewer bits per element
On a generic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ no index-calculus attack is known, so the best DLP algorithm is $O(\sqrt p)$ Pollard $\rho$ rather than sub-exponential
Elliptic curves have a faster group law than $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$
questions.explain: On generic elliptic curves no sub-exponential DLP algorithm is known, so a key length matching $O(\sqrt p)$ rather than $L_p[1/3]$ suffices. 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA/DH. (ECC is not post-quantum — Shor breaks it just as it breaks RSA.)

questions.q: By Hasse's theorem, the order $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ satisfies which bound?
questions.explain: Hasse: $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)=p+1-a_p$ with $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt p$. So the curve order is approximately $p$, with a $\sqrt p$-sized fluctuation.

questions.q: The MOV attack reduces the elliptic-curve discrete log on a curve $E/\mathbb{F}_p$ to the discrete log in which group?
choices: $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ for some embedding degree $k$, via the Weil pairing
questions.explain: The Weil/Tate pairing embeds $E[\ell]$ into $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ where $k$ is the smallest integer with $\ell\mid p^k-1$. When $k$ is small, sub-exponential index calculus on $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ beats $O(\sqrt p)$ on $E$. Standard curves (P-256, Curve25519) have $k$ astronomically large.

questions.q: Why is RSA / ECC considered "quantum-broken" in principle?
choices: Quantum computers can brute-force any cipher
Shor's algorithm factors integers and computes discrete logs in polynomial time on a fault-tolerant quantum computer
Quantum computers solve every NP problem in polynomial time
questions.explain: Shor (1994) gives polynomial-time quantum algorithms for integer factorization and the discrete log in any abelian group — so RSA, DH, and ECC all fall once a sufficiently large fault-tolerant quantum machine exists. Grover gives only a quadratic speedup on generic search, far weaker.

questions.q: The Learning With Errors (LWE) problem asks the solver to:
choices: Recover a secret vector $\mathbf{s}\in\mathbb{Z}_q^n$ from samples $(\mathbf{a}_i, \langle\mathbf{a}_i,\mathbf{s}\rangle + e_i \bmod q)$ where $e_i$ is small Gaussian noise
questions.explain: LWE is exactly noisy linear algebra mod $q$: without the error term you would solve for $\mathbf{s}$ by Gaussian elimination, but small Gaussian $e_i$ destroys that. Regev showed (quantum reduction) that average-case LWE is at least as hard as worst-case approximate-SVP / GapSVP.

questions.q: Which cryptosystem was selected by NIST in 2022/2024 as the primary post-quantum key-encapsulation standard (FIPS 203)?
questions.explain: CRYSTALS-Kyber, a Module-LWE KEM, became FIPS 203 in 2024. SIKE was a candidate but was broken in 2022 by the Castryck–Decru attack. Classic McEliece (code-based) and HQC are alternates; NTRU is closely related to Kyber but not the chosen primary standard.

questions.q: An interactive proof system has three properties. Which set is correct?
questions.explain: Completeness (true statements are accepted by an honest prover), soundness (false statements are rejected with high probability against any prover), and zero-knowledge (the verifier learns nothing beyond the truth of the statement — formalised by transcript simulatability).

questions.q: Schnorr's protocol proves knowledge of a discrete log $x$ with $h=g^x$. The prover sends $t=g^r$, the verifier sends a random challenge $c$, the prover replies with $s=r+cx$. The verifier accepts iff:
questions.explain: $g^s = g^{r+cx} = g^r \cdot (g^x)^c = t\cdot h^c$. Soundness: a prover who can answer two distinct challenges $c\ne c'$ for the same $t$ reveals $x=(s-s')/(c-c')$. Zero-knowledge: the simulator picks $s, c$ first and sets $t = g^s/h^c$.

questions.q: The acronym SNARK in zk-SNARK stands for which property set?
questions.explain: Succinct (proof size and verification time poly-logarithmic in the witness), Non-interactive (one message from prover to verifier, often via Fiat–Shamir), Argument of Knowledge (computational soundness, with an extractor pulling out a witness from any convincing prover). Groth16 and PLONK are the workhorses.
